title: Rabbit Pie
subtitle: Slow-braised wild rabbit in a mustard and cream sauce, under a puff pastry lid
excerpt: Rabbit is the most underused British meat: cheap, lean, and flavourful. Jointed and braised in white wine with mustard and cream, then set under a puff pastry lid, it makes a deeply satisfying pie that takes less effort than it suggests.
sourceNotes: Traditional British game cookery. Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): rabbit in mustard sauce. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: rabbit pie method. Traditional British farmhouse cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The braised filling must be made a day ahead and cooled completely before the pie is assembled.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven for the pastry lid

glossaryTerms.definition: Cutting a whole rabbit into portions at the joints: two back legs, two front legs, the saddle (back), and the rib section. Butchers will joint a rabbit on request; it takes about 5 minutes to do yourself with a heavy knife.

A French-derived sauce made by deglazing a pan with white wine, adding cream, and finishing with Dijon mustard off the heat. The mustard must be added at the end; prolonged cooking destroys its sharpness. It is the classic French treatment for rabbit and chicken.

content.text: Rabbit is a lean meat that benefits from the same approach as pheasant: a little fat added during cooking and a method that adds moisture. Ask the butcher to

the rabbit for you when you buy it. Here the fat comes from lardons fried at the start, and the moisture from a white wine and cream sauce. The result, after the rabbit has braised for 90 minutes, is meat that pulls from the bones with no resistance.
After braising, pick all the meat from the bones before filling the pie. The small bones of a rabbit leg are easy to find when you are looking for them, but embarrassing in a pie when you are not. A
finishes the braising liquid; the mustard goes in off the heat, not into the hot pan.

items.prepNote: jointed into 6-8 pieces by the butcher

content.text: in a casserole until golden. Remove and set aside. Brown the rabbit pieces in the same fat. Remove and set aside. Soften the onion for 5 minutes. Add garlic, thyme, and bay. Return the rabbit and lardons. Pour over

. Cover and braise over low heat or in a 160°C oven for 90 minutes until the meat pulls from the bones. Transfer the rabbit to a board, pick all the meat from the bones, and discard the bones. Return the meat to the braising liquid.
Make the sauce and fill the pie
and simmer for 5 minutes to reduce the sauce. Season with

. Cool completely and refrigerate until cold. Spoon into the pie dish. Lay the
over the top, press and trim the edges, score, cut a vent, and brush with beaten egg.
Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Bake for 35-40 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the filling is bubbling. Rest for 10 minutes before cutting.

rows.symptom: Meat is tough after 90 minutes of braising
rows.cause: Heat was too low, or the rabbit was older and needs longer
rows.fix: Continue braising in 20-minute increments. Wild rabbit can take up to 2 hours.

rows.cause: Lid was left off during braising, allowing too much evaporation
rows.fix: Braise with the lid on. If the sauce is thin when finished, reduce it by simmering uncovered for 10 minutes before adding cream.

rows.symptom: Small bones in the finished pie
rows.cause: Rabbit has many small rib and vertebral bones that are easy to miss
rows.fix: Work slowly and carefully when picking the meat. Run fingers through the shredded meat feeling for hard pieces.

rows.cause: Mustard was added while the sauce was still hot
rows.fix: Always add Dijon mustard off the heat; it loses its sharpness if cooked for more than a minute.

content.text: Rabbit pie appears in almost every British period cookery book from Mrs Beeton onwards. Wild rabbit was a staple protein in the British countryside for centuries, and the pie was the most reliable way to make a whole rabbit feed four people. Today it sits on the menu of gastropubs and farmhouse restaurants as a seasonal special, usually with a note about it being local wild rabbit.
